title: WH_KEYBOARD_LL keyboard-injection negative test
goal: Per spec §6.6.5 (AC-QUIET-INJECT-NEG-TEST) install a `WH_KEYBOARD_LL` low-level hook checking `KBDLLHOOKSTRUCT.flags & LLKHF_INJECTED`, fire `SendInput` shortcut sequences while a hidden Tauri window is alive, and assert (a) no `#[tauri::command]` handler fired, (b) no state mutation occurred, (c) the LL-hook saw the injection.
expected_diff_shape: New module src/backend/handshake_core/src/operator_foreground/keyboard_inject_test.rs (Windows-only); cargo test that orchestrates a hidden window + SendInput + assertions.
unblock_conditions: MT-014 quiet window + MT-015 focus audit merged so test can use them.
implementation_notes: Per spec §6.6.5 this is a *negative* test: it deliberately tries to drive Handshake via OS-level keyboard injection and proves the attempt cannot succeed. Cargo.toml: add `[target.'cfg(windows)'.dependencies] windows = { version = "<latest>", features = ["Win32_UI_WindowsAndMessaging", "Win32_UI_Input_KeyboardAndMouse"] }`. keyboard_inject_test.rs implements: (1) `install_ll_hook() -> LlHookHandle` calls `SetWindowsHookExW(WH_KEYBOARD_LL, callback, ...)` with a callback that examines `KBDLLHOOKSTRUCT.flags`; if `flags & LLKHF_INJECTED != 0`, increments an atomic injected_event_count. (2) `simulate_shortcut_injection(seq: &[VkSequence])` calls `SendInput` with the keystroke sequence (e.g., Ctrl+Shift+F to simulate an arbitrary registered shortcut). (3) `track_command_invocations(app: &AppHandle) -> CmdTracker` instruments every #[tauri::command] handler via a global counter (use a test-only middleware or a global AtomicU64 incremented in a test-only wrapping macro). The test flow: spawn a hidden QuietWindow, install LL hook, install cmd tracker, simulate injection of a known shortcut, sleep 100ms, query: assert injected_event_count >= 1 (the LL-hook saw the injection) AND assert tracker.invocation_count() == 0 (no Tauri command fired) AND assert state-mutation observable via a sentinel field == unchanged. Per HBR-QUIET-002 evidence_kind=automation_path_audit this satisfies the negative test obligation. Like MT-015, gated `#[cfg_attr(not(target_os = "windows"), ignore)]` and documented as Windows-desktop-only.
risk_if_missed: Without negative test, HBR-QUIET-002 'surface cannot be accidentally driven by global input hijacking' is unverified; a future regression could open Handshake to OS-keyboard hijack.
validator_focus: All three assertions (LL-hook saw injection, no Tauri command fired, no state mutation) are explicit and the test fails loudly on any one being violated.

minimum_controls: Test must assert the LL-hook actually saw the injection (proves the test is meaningful, not a no-op).
Cmd tracker uses a test-only macro to avoid leaking into release builds.
Test is gated to Windows-desktop and the gate reason is documented inline.
